title: Prefix Sum Range Query
question: You need to answer many range-sum queries on a static integer array. Which preprocessing technique gives O(1) query time after O(n) preprocessing?
explanation: A prefix sum array stores cumulative sums, so range sum l..r is prefix[r] - prefix[l - 1]. Sorting destroys original positions, a BST helps ordered lookup not range sums by index, and scanning each query is O(n).
sourceLine: You need to answer many range-sum queries on a static integer array. Which preprocessing technique gives O(1) query time after O(n) preprocessing?
referenceNote: Curated professional MCQ. Each option is designed to test a specific interview concept.

title: Next Greater Element Structure
question: Which data structure is commonly used to solve Next Greater Element in O(n) time?
explanation: A monotonic stack keeps unresolved elements in decreasing order and resolves them when a greater element appears. A queue does not maintain the needed ordering, a trie is for prefixes, and DSU is for connectivity.
sourceLine: Which data structure is commonly used to solve Next Greater Element in O(n) time?
referenceNote: Curated professional MCQ. Each option is designed to test a specific interview concept.

title: Two Sum Lookup
question: In the standard Two Sum problem, why is a hash map used?
options: To store complements and find matches in O(1) average time
To convert the problem into recursion
correctAnswer: To store complements and find matches in O(1) average time
explanation: A hash map stores previously seen values or complements for constant-average lookup. It does not sort, it uses O(n) memory, and recursion is unrelated to the optimal lookup pattern.
sourceLine: In the standard Two Sum problem, why is a hash map used?
referenceNote: Curated professional MCQ. Each option is designed to test a specific interview concept.

title: BFS Shortest Path
question: Why does BFS find the shortest path in an unweighted graph?
options: It always chooses the smallest edge weight
It explores nodes level by level from the source
correctAnswer: It explores nodes level by level from the source
explanation: In an unweighted graph, each BFS level represents one more edge from the source, so first visit gives shortest distance. It does not use edge weights, recursion depth is DFS-like, and sorted adjacency is not required.
sourceLine: Why does BFS find the shortest path in an unweighted graph?
referenceNote: Curated professional MCQ. Each option is designed to test a specific interview concept.

title: Dynamic Programming Signal
question: Which pair of properties usually indicates that dynamic programming may be useful?
options: Only constant memory and no choices
explanation: DP fits when subproblems repeat and optimal answers compose from smaller optimal answers. Sorted input hints binary search/two pointers, XOR hints bit tricks, and constant memory is an optimization not the reason for DP.
sourceLine: Which pair of properties usually indicates that dynamic programming may be useful?
referenceNote: Curated professional MCQ. Each option is designed to test a specific interview concept.

title: Union-Find Use Case
question: Which problem is best suited for Disjoint Set Union?
options: Detecting connected components while merging edges
correctAnswer: Detecting connected components while merging edges
explanation: DSU efficiently supports union and find operations for dynamic connectivity. Palindromes need string/DP methods, expressions need stacks/parsing, and LRU needs a hash map plus linked list.
sourceLine: Which problem is best suited for Disjoint Set Union?
referenceNote: Curated professional MCQ. Each option is designed to test a specific interview concept.

title: LRU Cache Design
question: What data structures are typically combined to implement an O(1) LRU cache?
options: Hash map and doubly linked list
correctAnswer: Hash map and doubly linked list
explanation: The hash map gives O(1) key lookup and the doubly linked list supports O(1) move-to-front and eviction from the tail. The other combinations do not support both lookup and recency updates in O(1).
sourceLine: What data structures are typically combined to implement an O(1) LRU cache?
referenceNote: Curated professional MCQ. Each option is designed to test a specific interview concept.

title: Dijkstra Limitation
question: Why is Dijkstra's algorithm unsafe on graphs with negative edge weights?
options: A node marked final may later receive a shorter path through a negative edge
It requires all edges to have weight one
correctAnswer: A node marked final may later receive a shorter path through a negative edge
explanation: Dijkstra assumes once the smallest tentative distance is extracted, it is final. Negative edges can violate that assumption. Priority queues are allowed, it works on general graphs, and unit weights are specifically the BFS case.
sourceLine: Why is Dijkstra's algorithm unsafe on graphs with negative edge weights?
referenceNote: Curated professional MCQ. Each option is designed to test a specific interview concept.
